stimulus:
Recyclable Plastic Yield (kg per tonne of input) from Pyrolysis and Solvolysis Methods at Two Plants

MethodLowest-yielding plant (kg/tonne)Highest-yielding plant (kg/tonne)
Pyrolysis612684
Solvolysis465540

Recycling mixed plastics into reusable material remains a challenge, in part because traditional mechanical processes degrade plastic quality. Two emerging chemical methods—pyrolysis and solvolysis—can break plastics down to their building blocks. Engineer Mariana Costa and colleagues compared the two methods at multiple commercial plants and concluded that pyrolysis is a more robust option for industrial-scale recycling of mixed plastics.
stem: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Costa and colleagues’ conclusion?

answerOptions.content: Even the lowest-yielding pyrolysis plant produced more recyclable plastic per tonne than the highest-yielding solvolysis plant did.
The highest-yielding pyrolysis plant produced approximately 684 kg of recyclable plastic per tonne, while the highest-yielding solvolysis plant produced approximately 540 kg per tonne.
Both methods showed a substantial difference between their lowest- and highest-yielding plants.
The lowest-yielding plant overall used solvolysis, while the highest-yielding plant overall used pyrolysis.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because it shows that pyrolysis outperforms solvolysis even in worst-case versus best-case comparison: the weakest pyrolysis plant (612 kg/tonne) still beat the strongest solvolysis plant (540 kg/tonne). This robustly supports the conclusion that pyrolysis is more dependable industrially. Choice B reports values without comparing methods directly in the way needed. Choice C describes within-method variation, not between-method advantage. Choice D restates extremes without showing the lowest pyrolysis exceeds the highest solvolysis.
